page:
## Life in the Abyss
Below 200 meters, sunlight disappears — yet the deep sea teems with life adapted to crushing pressure, near-freezing temperatures, and perpetual darkness.
### Topics
- **Hydrothermal vents and chemosynthesis** — mid-ocean ridge vent fields; sulfur-oxidizing bacteria as primary producers; tube worms (*Riftia pachyptila*), vent crabs, and alvinellid worms; why vents were among the most startling biological discoveries of the 20th century - **Abyssal plain communities and adaptations to pressure** — sea cucumbers, brittle stars, and xenophyophores; piezophilic enzymes and pressure-adapted cell membranes; how the abyssal plain depends almost entirely on marine snow from above - **Bioluminescence and deep-sea gigantism** — chemical light production (luciferin/luciferase); uses for predation, camouflage, and communication; gigantism in isopods, squid, and other taxa and the hypotheses explaining it
### Key Questions
1. How does chemosynthesis allow vent communities to thrive without any connection to sunlight? 2. Why are deep-sea organisms so vulnerable to disturbance despite living in such an extreme environment? 3. What are the biological and ethical arguments for and against deep-sea mining?
### Activity
Virtual deep dive: Explore footage from a remotely operated vehicle (ROV) expedition. Identify at least five organisms and hypothesize how each adapts to one specific deep-sea challenge.
